dbd3145e-3552-4c2d-ad4f-966517d78b3f.exp: Light microscopy does not show obvious glomerular lesions in minimal change disease. Immunofluorescent microscopy is usually negative for deposits, occasionally it may show deposits of small amount of IgM in the mesangium. Electron microscopy shows effacement of foot process suppoing the epithelial podocytes with weakening of slit pores. Minimal change disease presents clinically with the abrupt onset of edema and nephrotic syndrome. Average protein excretion is 10gm/24hrs. Less common features are hypeension, microscopic hematuria, atopy, and decreased renal function. Patients with nephrotic syndrome are at increased risk of thromboembolic events, develop severe hyperlipidemia, and may experience protein malnutrition. Ref: Harrison's Principles of Internal Medicine 18e chapter 283.

0ee475aa-b972-4b43-9b13-81b126cb1266.exp: .case fatality ratio is calculated as the total number of deaths due to a paicular disease divided by total number of cases due to the same disease ,whol;e multiplied by 100 that is 10/50 *100 equals 20% .

9c7b39f1-a42e-47d5-a72f-563274faeabb.exp: In addition to its carcinogenic propeies, DES is a known teratogen, an agent capable of causing malformations in daughters and sons who were exposed in utero. DES-exposed daughters are at an increased risk of abnormalities of the reproductive tract, including vaginal epithelial changes such as vaginal adenosis (which means a type of cell normally found in the uterus, columnar cells, are also present in the vagina), an increased cervical transformation zone, and uterine abnormalities, such as T-shaped uterus.

dbacfa86-9a0a-4ffb-b22a-ccc934e84fa3.exp: Bumetanide is most potent loop diuretic Torsemide is longest acting loop diuretic.

02f902a4-b950-477d-9a01-c959293dbae3.exp: The risk of fetal infection depends on the degree of maternal spirochetemia (greater in the secondary stage than in the primary or latent stages) and the gestational age of the fetus. Treponemes may cross the placenta at all stages of pregnancy, but fetal involvement is rare before 18 weeks. After 18 weeks, the fetus is able to mount an immunologic response, and tissue damage may result. The earlier in pregnancy the fetus is exposed, the more severe the fetal infection and the greater the risk of premature delivery or stillbih. Ref: Mackay G. (2013). Chapter 43. Sexually Transmitted Diseases & Pelvic Infections. In A.H. DeCherney, L. Nathan, N. Laufer, A.S. Roman (Eds), CURRENT Diagnosis & Treatment: Obstetrics & Gynecology, 11e.

bcbd64c4-c0da-4bed-afc5-9bfa9ef1ec29.exp: Ans. D i.e. Rheumatic fever Rheumatic fever It is an inflammatory disease that occurs following a Streptococcus pyogenes infection, such as streptococcal pharyngitis or scarlet fever. Believed to be caused by antibody cross-reactivity that can involve the hea, joints, skin, and brain, the illness typically develops two to three weeks after a streptococcal infection. Acute rheumatic fever commonly appears in children between the ages of 6 and 15, with only 20% of first-time attacks occurring in adults. The illness is so named because of its similarity in presentation to rheumatism. Mitral valve is commonly affected followed by aoic valve.

9367d96b-e809-48e4-904a-629f5a8bbb54.exp: Ans. D. Serum Na+ and K+ levelsMagnesium sulphate is drug of choice for eclampsia. It has no detrimental effects on neonate within therapeutic level.It reduces motor end plate sensitivity to acetyl choline.It induces cerebral vasodilatation, dilates uterine arteries increases production of endothelial prostacyclin and inhibits platelet activation.RegimenLoading doseMaintenance dosePritchard4gm IV over 3-4 min, 10mg deep IM5gm IM in alternate buttockZuspan4gm IV over 5-10min1-2gm/hour IV infusionSibai6gm IV over 20min2gm/hour IV infusionRepeat injections are given only if knee jerks are present, urine output exceeds 30ml/hour, and respiration rate is more than 12/min. The therapeutic level of serum magnesium is 4--7mEq/L.

da4886b9-3111-4714-9876-07e6460acb37.exp: Epiblepharon comprises an extra horizontal fold of skin stretching across the anterior lid margin. It can be seen in hypeelorism and congenital epicanthus. Since it is a congenital condition, it has no relation to trauma fold of skin and conjunctiva turned inwards. Usually resolves within the first few years of life. Surgery indicated if it persists or there is significant corneal trauma due to the associated trichiasis. Surgery involves removal of the excess fold of skin

f62e0afd-05e3-4d5e-8581-7791516a5ca6.exp: Other causes of haemorrhagic Conjunctivitis are Echo virus -34 Bacterial causes are Hemophilus and pneumococcus REF:Refer Khurana 6th edition page number 76

e0bb7d65-a5db-42e0-b434-f35870327476.exp: Prophylactic antibiotic: single dose 30 to 60 minutes before skin incision (and not at the time of incision) Cefazolin 1 to 2 g and increased to 3 g for obese patients is recommended. Re-dosing is recommended after 3 hours of surgery and with excessive bleeding. Chlorhexidine-alcohol-based skin preparations have a lower odds of surgical site infection compared with povidone-iodine preparations. Concentrations of 4% or less can be used in the vagina Although treating all patients with bacterial vaginosis may decrease vaginal cuff cellulitis, universal screening preoperatively is not recommended as a routine.

7939643d-fc58-4989-af69-1fd7aa9ef951.exp: Ans. is 'a* i.e., Urine retention AUTOSOMAL DOMINANT POLYCYSTIC KIDNEY DISEASEo Autosomal dominant polycystic disease is characterized by B/L involvement of the kidney.Pathplogyo Both the kidney are grossly enlarged.o Multiple cysts cover the surface of the kidney.o The cysts contain straw coloured fluid that may become hemorrhagic.o The cysts are distributed evenly throughout the cortex and medullae. (cysts can also occcur in extrarenal locations such as spleen liver).Clinical featuress :o Chronic flank pain d/t mass effect of kidney.o Acute abdominal pain indicates the presence of urinary tract infection.o Gross and microscopic hematuria is common.o Urinary- tract infection is common and may involve the bladder or the renal interstitium l pyelonephritis) or infect a cyst (pyocyst).o Nephrolithiasis occurs in 15-20% of the patients. Factors contributing to nephrolithiasis are low urine pH, low urine citrate and urinary stasis and from distortion of the collecting system by cysts.o Hypertension is found in 20% to 30% of children and upto 75% of adults. (It is secondary to intrarenal ischemia from distortion of the renal architecture).o Progressive decline in renal function is common with - 50% of patients developing ESRDdeg by age 60. ESRD is approached more rapidly in patients with hypertension.

0a2b5e0e-f462-4f88-ba1a-117d18d627a9.exp: A child born to mother who was diagnosed to have TB in pregnancy should receive prophylaxis for 6 months, provided congenital TB has been ruled out

2e986cd8-54e9-4a10-a95b-f7c18eff9432.exp: Acute osteomyelitis is almost invariably a disease of children. The causative organism is usually staphylococcus aureus ( 60-80% of cases), less often streptococcus pyogenes and streptococcus pneumoniae. In children Patients with sickle cell disease are prone to infection by salmonella. Metaphysis is common site of osteomyelitis because of hairpin arrangements of blood vessels, Rapid growth in the region, Defective phagocytosis in the region and metaphyseal hemorrhage due to trauma in the region. The lower femoral metaphysis is commonest site for osteomyelitis. Sequestrum is a piece of dead bone, surrounded by a infected granulation tissue. Involucrum is a dense sclerotic bone overlying sequestrum. Ref: Apley's 8/e, Page 28,32; Ebnezar Textbook of Ohopedics By John Ebnezar 4/e, Page 541-42; Maheswari 3/e, Page 157-162.

abca6ca9-156e-4db9-9135-4ed5c6ae5577.exp: Ans. is 'a' i.e., Thumb adduction o Normally when a person is asked to grasp a book between the thumb and index finger, he will grasp the book firmly vdth thumb extended., taking full advantage of the adductor pollicis and the first dorsal interosseous muscles,o If the ulnar nerve is injured the adductor pollicis will be paralysed and the patient will hold the book by using the flexor pollicislongus (supplied by median nerve) producing flexion at the interphalangeal joint,o This becomes more pronounced if the examiner tries to pull the book out while the patient tries to hold it.o This sign is known as Troment's sign' or the 'book test'.

e675442f-9abc-4a0f-8242-8cae12d48d61.exp:
USG is the imaging modality of choice for evaluation of infantile pyloric stenosis. It is both highly sensitive (90-99%) and specific (95-100%) in the hands of a qualified sonographer.
USG findings of infantile pyloric stenosis are :

               Target sign             - Pyloric muscle wall thickness → > 4mm
               Cervix sign             - Pyloric length (segment length) → ≥ 16mm
               Antral nipple sign    - Pyloric diameter a → ≥ 15mm

f209eece-c63a-4dcd-b8ee-1b31ab492a59.exp: Ans. a. Pseudomonas aeruginosa (non mucoid) Most common organism associated with cystic fibrosis is Pseudomonas aeruginosa (non mucoid) among the given options.Pulmonary infections in Cystic Fibrosis: Persistent colonization/ infection with typical cystic fibrosis pathogens, includingStaphylococcus aureus (MC in children(r)), nontypeable, Hemophilus influenzae, mucoid and nonmucoid Pseudomonas aeruginosa (MC in adults and overall(r)), Burkholderia cepase."Characteristic Classical Manifestations of Cystic FibrosisRespiratory system(Sinopulmonary disease)Gastrointestinal System(Intestinal, Hepatobiliary, Pancreatic)Genitourinary System(Male Infertility)* Pulmonary infection: Persistent colonization/ infection with typical cystic fibrosis pathogens, including Staphylococcus aureus (MC in children), nontypeable, Hemophilus influenzae, mucoid and nonmucoid Pseudomonas aeruginosa (MC in adults and overall0), Burkholderia cepase* Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease* Bronchiectasis0* Airway obstruction manifested by wheezing and air trapping* Chronic cough and sputum production* Digital clubbing* Persistent chest radiograph abnormalities (e.g. Bronchiectasis, atelectasis, infiltrates, hyperinflation)* Nasal Polyps* Radiographic abnormalities of paranasal sinuses* Intestinal: Meconium ileus, distal intestinal obstruction syndrome, rectal prolapse.* Pancreatic: Pancreatic exocrine deficiency* Hepatobiliary: Hepatic disease manifested by clinical or histologic evidence of focal biliary cirrhosis, or multilobular cirrhosis* Male urogenital abnormalities resulting in obstructive azoospermia are typical* Nutritional: Failure to thrive (protein-calone malnutrition), hypoproteinemia, edema, complications secondary to fat-soluble vitamin deficiency Metabolic AbnormalitiesCharacterized by salt depletion syndrome.* Hyponatremia* Hypocholeremia* Hypokalemia* Metabolic alkalosis

e58516d5-02c9-44a8-a46b-1fa239c4771a.exp: The patient is manifesting symptoms of malignant hypehermia (fevers, rigors, and myoglobinuria), which is treated by administration of dantrolene, immediate discontinuation of offending medication (which can include succinylcholine or halothanebased inhalational anesthetics), and suppoive cooling measures. While urine alkalinization, loop diuretics, and mannitol are appropriate treatment measures for rhabdomyolysis, the underlying problem in this patient is malignant hypehermia, which, because of its associated moality of 30% in severe cases, should be treated first and foremost. Malignant hypehermia is not a manifestation of anaphylactic shock, and therefore steroids and antihistamines have no role in its treatment.

37d9ca13-6b2e-46e9-84ff-6a9d7187bd60.exp: ANS. A# Mechanism of action of cell wall synthesis inhibitors* Beta lactam antibiotics: Transpeptidase blocker* Vancomycin: Transglycosylation blocker* Dephosphorylation blocker: Bacitracin* Enol pyruvate transferase blocker: Fosfomycin* Alanine ligase blocker: Cycloserine# Mechanism of action of protein synthesis inhibitors* Aminoglycoside: Blocks initiation by binding to 30s* Linezolid: Blocks initiation by binding to 50s* Tetracycline: Blocks attachment of new amino acid to acceptor site* Chloramphenicol: Peptidyltransferase blocker* Macrolide, lincosamide, streptogramin: Blocks translocation

e2b23b24-f3e0-4048-9dd2-1a010bc8f96b.exp: Ans is 'a' i.e. Metaphysis Repeat from May 09Acute osteomyelitis is almost invariably a disease of children. When adults are affected it may be because their resistance is lowered by debility, disease or drugs.Acute osteomyelitis may result from hematogenous spread of the infecting organism, or may be infected secondarily from the spread of a contiguous area of infection, or direct inoculation of bacteria in the bone (open fractures, nail punctures of the foot).Hematogenous spread is the commonest means of production of osteomyelitis.Commonest site is metaphysis. This is because of the peculiar arrangement of the blood vessels in that area, which leads to relative vascular stasis, favouring bacterial colonization. - Commonest site: Lower femoral metaphyses > Upper tibial, upper femoral, upper humeral metaphyses.Acute hematogenous osteomyelitis behaves differently in neonates from the way it does in children. Because blood vessels cross the growth plate in neonates and infants younger than age 18 months, the bone infection that develops in that age group will likely cross the physis; however, in older children, acute infections rarely cross the growth plate.In adults, hematogenous infection is more common in the vertebrae than in the long bones.

4709593d-92f6-45fb-9a88-a1cab88fd871.exp: Ans. is 'c' i.e., MacrophagesAcute inflammatory reaction with a predominance of Neutrophils is seen upto 2-3 days.After 3 days (i.e. after 72 hours), Macrophages become the predominant cell type and become involved in removal of necrotic debris.

6a20e9bc-77dd-479b-9219-d62eabd24bd7.exp:
Both ABC and osteoblastoma can arise in the posterior elements of vertebrae (i.e. Pedicle, lamina, transverse process or spinous process).
ABC produces lytic (lucent) lesion, while osteoblastoma produces lytic lesion surrounded by a rim of sclerosis.
